attributes.stem:
A 35-year-old previously healthy man presents to the Emergency Department with a four hour history of rapid onset severe, cramp-like abdominal pain and bloating. He feels nauseated and has vomited once. During the physical examination, the physician notices pigmentation on the patient's lips (image), which the patient recalls has been present since childhood. The abdomen is uniformly distended, with mild generalised tenderness. Increased high-pitched bowel sounds are evident on auscultation.

A computed tomography scan of the abdomen and pelvis is performed, revealing the presence of several loops of dilated small bowel and the appearance of a target sign, consistent with a small intestinal obstruction.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely underlying cause?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with symptoms consistent with Peutz-Jeghers syndrome (PJS), an autosomal dominant syndrome characterised by the presence of hamartomatous polyps in the gastrointestinal tract, mucocutaneous pigmentation, and an increased risk of malignancy. The pigmentation seen on the patient's lips and oral region is a typical finding in this syndrome. Pigmented macules may also appear on the palms and soles, but they tend to fade after puberty.

Hamartomatous polyps are most commonly found in the small bowel (60-90% of cases), but they can also occur in the colon and stomach. While many individuals with PJS are asymptomatic at the time of diagnosis, the polyps can act as a pathological lead point and cause complications such as intussusception, as observed in this patient. The "target sign" seen on imaging is a characteristic finding in cases of intussusception, representing layers of the intestine folding into one another.

Intussusception is a common gastrointestinal complication of Henoch-Schönlein purpura (HSP), also known as IgA vasculitis, due to oedema and haemorrhage in the bowel mucosa acting as a pathological lead point. However, HSP is typically diagnosed during childhood and is associated with palpable purpura over weight-bearing areas such as the buttocks and lower extremities. Dark pigmented macules are not a characteristic feature of this condition.

Intussusception rarely presents as the initial manifestation of Crohn's disease. Crohn's disease is also not associated with the hyperpigmentation observed on the patient's lips.

Malignant melanoma of the oral cavity is a rare condition and carries an extremely poor prognosis. Cutaneous melanoacanthoma, a benign epithelial lesion, typically presents as a solitary black plaque or nodule on the head, neck, or trunk of elderly individuals. While both of these lesions can resemble the pigmentation seen in the patient's lips, it is unlikely for them to manifest since young and present with bowel symptoms.
learningPoints: Peutz-Jeghers syndrome (PJS) is an autosomal dominant condition marked by hamartomatous polyps, mucocutaneous pigmentation, and increased cancer risk.
references: Beggs AD, Latchford AR, Vasen HF, Moslein G, Alonso A, Aretz S, et al. Peutz–Jeghers syndrome: a systematic review and recommendations for management. Gut [Internet]. 2010 Jul 1 [cited 2023 Jul 13];59(7):975-86. Available from: https://gut.bmj.com/content/59/7/975 doi: 10.1136/gut.2009.198499

Wang H, Luo T, Liu WQ, Huang Y, Wu XT, Wang XJ. Clinical presentations and surgical approach of acute intussusception caused by peutz–jeghers syndrome in adults. J Gastrointest Surg [Internet]. 2011 Dec [cited 2023 Jul 13];15:2218-25. Available from: https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s11605-011-1724-2 doi: 10.1007/s11605-011-1724-2
